name: Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST)
summary: AST is an enzyme found in the liver, but also in heart, skeletal muscle, kidney, and red blood cells. Tissue injury releases AST into the bloodstream, so it is a sensitive but less liver-specific marker than ALT.
whatItMeans: Elevated AST suggests cell injury that can originate in liver, heart, or muscle. Because it is not liver-specific, AST is interpreted alongside ALT: an AST-to-ALT ratio greater than 2 classically suggests alcoholic liver disease, while a ratio greater than 1 can also indicate advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis. An isolated AST rise with a normal ALT may point to a muscle or red-cell (hemolysis) source rather than the liver, which is why creatine kinase is often checked alongside it. This is educational information, not a diagnosis.
raises: Liver injury (hepatitis, fatty liver, alcohol, drug toxicity)
Alcoholic liver disease (often AST greater than ALT)
Skeletal muscle injury, rhabdomyolysis, or intense exercise
lowers: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) deficiency can lower measured AST activity
Resolution of the underlying tissue injury
confounders: Intense or unaccustomed exercise raises AST (muscle source)
Creatine supplementation and high muscle mass can mildly raise AST
Hemolyzed blood samples falsely elevate AST
Vitamin B6 deficiency falsely lowers AST
AST is found in many tissues, so elevation is not liver-specific

ranges.note: General adult reference range; varies by lab, age, and sex.
Typical adult male upper limit (~35-40 U/L).
Typical adult female upper limit (~25-30 U/L).

references.title: Mayo Clinic Press: My liver enzymes are elevated, now what?
